questions.question_text: Explain the four pillars of Object Oriented Programming.
questions.sample_answer: The four pillars of Object Oriented Programming are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction. Encapsulation bundles data and methods within a class and restricts direct access to internal state. Inheritance allows a child class to acquire properties and methods from a parent class, enabling code reuse. Polymorphism allows the same method or interface to behave differently depending on the object type. Abstraction hides implementation complexity and exposes only the necessary interface to the user.
follow_up_hints: Can you give a real-world example of polymorphism?
How does encapsulation help with code maintenance?
What is the difference between abstract class and interface?

questions.question_text: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
questions.sample_answer: An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated directly and may contain both abstract methods (without implementation) and concrete methods (with implementation). A subclass must implement all abstract methods. An interface defines a contract of methods that a class must implement but contains no implementation itself. The key difference is that a class can implement multiple interfaces but typically inherits from only one abstract class. Abstract classes are used when classes share common behavior while interfaces define capabilities.
follow_up_hints: When would you choose abstract class over interface?
How does Python handle interfaces differently from Java?

questions.question_text: What is the difference between a stack and a queue? Give examples of where each is used.
questions.sample_answer: A stack is a LIFO (Last In First Out) data structure where the last element added is the first to be removed. Operations are push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). Use cases include function call stacks, undo operations, and expression parsing. A queue is a FIFO (First In First Out) data structure where the first element added is the first to be removed. Operations are enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front). Use cases include task scheduling, print queues, and breadth-first search.
follow_up_hints: How would you implement a queue using two stacks?
What is a priority queue and when would you use it?

questions.question_text: Explain Big O notation and give examples of common time complexities.
questions.sample_answer: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time or space complexity as the input size grows. O(1) is constant time, meaning execution time does not depend on input size, for example accessing an array element by index. O(n) is linear time, where execution time grows proportionally with input size, for example iterating through an array. O(log n) is logarithmic, where execution time grows logarithmically, for example binary search. O(n^2) is quadratic, where execution time grows with the square of input size, for example bubble sort.
follow_up_hints: What is the time complexity of sorting algorithms?
How would you optimize an O(n^2) algorithm?

questions.question_text: What are ACID properties in database transactions?
questions.sample_answer: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. Atomicity means a transaction is treated as a single unit, either all operations succeed or all are rolled back. Consistency means a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another, maintaining all rules and constraints. Isolation means concurrent transactions execute as if they were sequential, preventing dirty reads and phantom reads. Durability means once a transaction is committed, the changes are permanently saved even in the event of a system failure.
follow_up_hints: What isolation levels exist in SQL?
What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?

questions.question_text: Explain REST principles and the purpose of different HTTP methods.
questions.sample_answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for APIs based on stateless communication over HTTP. Key principles include statelessness (each request contains all necessary information), uniform interface, and resource-based URLs. HTTP methods define operations on resources: GET retrieves data without side effects, POST creates new resources, PUT updates or replaces existing resources, DELETE removes resources. PATCH partially updates a resource. Status codes communicate results: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.
follow_up_hints: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
How do you handle API versioning?

questions.question_text: How would you design a URL shortening service like bit.ly?
questions.sample_answer: A URL shortening service requires a system that maps short codes to long URLs. The design includes: an API layer to handle create and redirect requests, a hash generation service to create unique short codes (using base62 encoding of an auto-incremented ID), a database to store the mapping, and a cache layer like Redis to speed up redirects for popular URLs. For scalability, we use a load balancer to distribute traffic, database sharding for data distribution, and CDN for geographic distribution. The redirect operation uses HTTP 301 (permanent) or 302 (temporary) redirects.
follow_up_hints: How would you handle hash collisions?
How would you scale to handle millions of requests per second?

questions.question_text: Tell me about yourself and your technical background.
questions.sample_answer: I am a software engineering graduate with strong foundations in Python and web development. I have worked on several projects including a REST API built with FastAPI and a machine learning pipeline for data classification. My core skills include Python, SQL, and basic DevOps with Docker. I am passionate about building scalable systems and continuously improving my knowledge. I am particularly interested in backend engineering and distributed systems, which is why I am excited about this role.
follow_up_hints: What project are you most proud of?
Why are you transitioning from your current role?

questions.question_text: Describe a time you faced a significant technical challenge and how you resolved it.
questions.sample_answer: In a university project, I was building a REST API that began returning slow responses as we added more data. I analyzed the issue using query logs and found that several database queries lacked indexes and were performing full table scans. I added appropriate indexes, rewrote two N+1 query patterns to use joins, and added Redis caching for frequently accessed data. The result was a 10x improvement in response times from 3 seconds to 300 milliseconds. This taught me to always profile before optimizing and to think about database design early in the project.
follow_up_hints: What would you do differently next time?
How did the team respond to your solution?

questions.question_text: Where do you see yourself in 5 years professionally?
questions.sample_answer: In five years I see myself as a senior software engineer with deep expertise in distributed systems and backend architecture. I want to grow from writing individual features to designing systems that scale to millions of users. I also aim to mentor junior engineers and contribute to technical decisions. I am particularly interested in deepening my knowledge of cloud infrastructure and system design. This role excites me because it provides exposure to real-world scalability challenges that will accelerate that growth.
